problem: Let $P(x) = (x-1)(x-2)(x-3)$. For how many polynomials $Q(x)$ does there exist a polynomial $R(x)$ of degree 3 such that $P\left(Q(x)\right) = P(x)\cdot R(x)$?

classified_questions.question: Can you describe the relationship between $P(x)$ and $Q(x)$ given the equation $P(Q(x)) = P(x) \cdot R(x)$?
classified_questions.label: r

classified_questions.question: What is the degree of the polynomial $Q(x)$ in order for $P(Q(x))$ to be equal to $P(x) \cdot R(x)$? Why?
classified_questions.label: r

classified_questions.question: How many choices are there for $(Q(1), Q(2), Q(3))$ based on the given information?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Which choices of $(Q(1), Q(2), Q(3))$ yield polynomials $Q(x)$ with a degree less than 2? Why?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: For the remaining choices of $(Q(1), Q(2), Q(3))$, what is the degree of the polynomial $Q(x)$? Why?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How many choices of $(Q(1), Q(2), Q(3))$ yield non-collinear points? Why?
classified_questions.label: b
